interview_metadata.name: Deeva Cintron
interview_metadata.protocol: American Voices Project (Park et al. 2024, Table 7) — Full 109 questions

transcript.question_text: To start, I would like to begin with a big question: tell me the story of your life. Start from the beginning -- from your childhood, to education, to family and relationships, and to any major life events you may have had.
transcript.response: Well, where do I even begin? I was born in 1941, right here in Michigan, in a little town not far from where I live now. My parents were good, solid people -- my father's folks came over from Germany, and my mother's side too, so we had that sort of practical, no-nonsense upbringing. We grew up on a small piece of land, not exactly a farm but we always had a big garden, chickens, that sort of thing. My mother canned everything -- green beans, tomatoes, sauerkraut. I learned all of that from her, and I still do it to this day. My father worked hard, came home tired, but on Sundays we'd all go to services together and then have a big dinner. That was just how it was done. I had two brothers, both older than me. I was the baby and the only girl, so I learned early on to hold my own. I went to the local schools, did pretty well -- I always liked arithmetic and keeping things organized. After high school I went to the community college for a couple of years, studying business administration. I wanted to finish, I really did, but then I met Harold at a church social in 1961 and, well, things moved pretty quickly in those days. We got married in 1962, and I put school on hold. Harold got a job at the Ford plant, and I stayed home with the children. We had three -- two boys and a girl. I threw myself into keeping the house running like clockwork. Budgets, schedules, meal plans, the whole nine yards. I did the books for Harold's side work too, little carpentry jobs he'd pick up on weekends. When the kids got older, I started volunteering at the church -- organizing fellowship dinners, managing the treasury for the women's group. That kept me busy for decades. We moved to Saline in 1978, bought the house we're still in, and I've been here ever since. Lost my mother in 1995, my father a few years before that. My brothers are both gone now too. That's been hard, watching everyone from that generation go. But Harold and I, we've kept each other going. Eighty-five years, and here I am, still puttering around the garden and double-checking my crossword answers.

follow_ups.question: You mentioned canning and gardening from your mother. What does your garden look like these days, and how has that tradition stayed with you?
follow_ups.response: Oh, it's smaller than it used to be, I'll tell you that. I can't bend down like I could at fifty. But I still have my raised beds -- Harold built those for me about ten years ago when my knees started giving me trouble. I grow carrots, tomatoes, some herbs like rosemary and thyme, and I always put in a couple rows of cabbage for sauerkraut. I still can a batch every fall. It's not as much as my mother used to do, but it connects me to her. I go out every morning after breakfast, check the beds, pull weeds. It's the first thing I do, rain or shine, as long as it's not winter.

follow_ups.question: You said you put school on hold when you married Harold. Has that ever come back up for you?
follow_ups.response: It has, actually. I've always wished I'd finished. I was so close -- just needed maybe another year's worth of classes to get my certificate in business administration. A few years ago, one of my grandchildren told me some of those courses are online now. I've been thinking about it, honestly. I'm pretty good with email and I can do a video call, though it takes me a minute. I'd love to finish, not for a career, but just to say I did it. And I think it would help me when I'm helping the younger ones figure out their budgets and finances. I like being the one people come to for that sort of thing.

follow_ups.question: What was it like raising three children while Harold worked at the Ford plant?
follow_ups.response: Busy, but I liked having a system. I had the week planned out -- Monday was laundry, Tuesday was groceries, and so on. The boys were a handful, always running around, but my daughter was more like me, liked things neat and orderly. Harold worked long shifts, so during the week it was really just me running the show. I kept a ledger for every penny we spent. When Harold brought home his check, I'd sit down at the kitchen table that same evening and divide it all up -- mortgage, groceries, gas, savings. We never had a lot extra, but we never missed a payment either. I'm proud of that.

transcript.question_text: Some people tell us that they've reached a crossroads at some points in their life where multiple paths were available, and their choice then made a significant difference in defining who they are. What about you? Was there a moment like that for you, and if so, could you tell me the whole story about that from start to finish?
transcript.response: I'd say the biggest crossroads was when I had to decide between finishing my college courses and marrying Harold. I was twenty years old, had a year or so left, and I was enjoying the classes. But Harold was a good man, steady, and in those days you didn't wait around. My mother told me, 'A good man doesn't come along twice.' And she was right about Harold, I'll give her that. So I chose the marriage and the family. I don't regret it, not one bit, but I do sometimes wonder what might have been different if I'd finished first. Maybe I would have worked in an office, done bookkeeping professionally. I think I would have been good at it. The other crossroads was in 1978 when we decided to move to Saline. Harold's plant was shifting, and we could have gone closer to Detroit or stayed put in our old town. But Saline had good schools for the kids and it felt safe, like the kind of place where you know your neighbors. We stretched ourselves to buy the house, and there were a few tight years, but it turned out to be the best decision we ever made. This is home.

follow_ups.question: When you were making the decision to move to Saline, how did you and Harold work through that together?
follow_ups.response: Harold and I sat down at the kitchen table with a notepad and I wrote out two columns -- reasons to move, reasons to stay. I added up what we'd spend on the mortgage versus what we were paying in rent. Harold was more of a gut-feeling person, but I needed the numbers to line up. In the end, the numbers said we could just barely do it, and Harold's gut said yes, so we went for it. We drove out here on a Sunday afternoon, walked around the neighborhood, and I said, 'This is it.' I could picture the kids riding bikes on these streets.

follow_ups.question: Looking back, do you feel the move to Saline shaped the kind of community life you've had?
follow_ups.response: Oh absolutely. Saline is where I found my people. I got involved with the church right away, started helping with the potlucks and then the treasury. I joined the library volunteer program. I met my bridge group here. If we'd moved closer to Detroit, I don't think I would have had the same kind of tight-knit community. Saline is small enough that you see the same faces at the grocery store, at the senior center, at the library. That matters to me. I like knowing where I fit.

transcript.question_text: Some people tell us they made a conscious choice or decision in moments like these, while others say it 'just happened'. What about you?
transcript.response: I'm a planner, so I'd say it was always a conscious choice. Even marrying Harold -- people might say, oh, you just fell in love, but I thought it through. I weighed what my life would look like either way. I'm the kind of person who writes things down, makes lists, checks them twice. I don't just let things happen to me if I can help it. That said, sometimes the timing of things -- like meeting Harold at that particular church social -- that part was just life happening. But what I did with it, that was deliberate.

follow_ups.question: Does that planning tendency show up in other parts of your life too?
follow_ups.response: In every part. I plan my meals for the week on Sunday afternoon. I have a calendar on the kitchen wall with every appointment, every volunteer shift, every bridge game written in. If something isn't on the calendar, it might as well not exist. Harold teases me about it, but he also knows exactly where to look when he forgets what day the dentist is.

transcript.question_text: Do you think another person or an organization could have lent a helping hand during moments like this?
transcript.response: You know, when I left school, if there had been some kind of program that let married women finish their courses part-time, that would have made a world of difference. Back then, once you got married and started a family, that was it. The college didn't have evening classes or anything flexible. Now they have all these online options, and I think that's wonderful. I also think if someone at the church had said, 'Deeva, you should go back and finish,' that would have given me a push. But nobody thought that way in the sixties. You did what was expected.

follow_ups.question: Do you think that experience shaped how you encouraged your own children or grandchildren about education?
follow_ups.response: Definitely. I told all three of my kids, finish your degree before you make any big decisions. My daughter listened -- she got her bachelor's. The boys, well, they each did things their own way, but I always said, 'Don't leave it half done like I did.' And my grandchildren, I'm the same way with them. I want them to have that piece of paper, that sense of completion.

transcript.question_text: Moving to present time, tell me more about family who are important to you. Do you have a partner, or children?
transcript.response: Harold is still here with me, thank the Lord. We've been married sixty-four years now. He's eighty-seven, moves a little slower than he used to, but he's still sharp. He putters around the garage, fixes things, watches his ball games. We have a nice routine together. Our oldest boy, Robert, he's sixty-two. He lives over in Ann Arbor, about twenty minutes away, so we see him fairly often. He brings groceries sometimes or helps Harold with things around the house. Our second, James, is fifty-nine. He's down in Ohio, so we don't see him as much, but he calls every Sunday without fail. And our daughter, Patricia, she's fifty-six and lives in Ypsilanti. She's the one who checks in on me the most -- she'll stop by twice a week, sometimes more. She worries. I tell her I'm fine, but I think it makes her feel better. We have seven grandchildren between the three of them, ranging from late teens to mid-thirties. A couple of the older ones have little ones of their own now, so Harold and I are great-grandparents three times over. They bring the babies by on holidays, and that's just the most wonderful thing. Family is what keeps us going at this age, honestly. Without them, the days would be very quiet.

follow_ups.question: How do you stay in touch with the grandchildren and great-grandchildren who don't live nearby?
follow_ups.response: Well, I've learned to do email, and Patricia helped me set up video calls on the computer. I'm not fast at it, but I can manage. The grandchildren will send me photos of the babies, and I print them out and put them on the refrigerator. I also write actual letters sometimes -- the older grandchildren think that's funny, getting something in the mailbox that isn't a bill. But I like writing by hand. It feels more personal. And at holidays, everyone comes to our house for dinner. That's the big gathering, and I start planning the menu weeks in advance.

follow_ups.question: You mentioned Harold watches his ball games. What does a typical evening look like for the two of you?
follow_ups.response: After supper -- and we eat early, around five o'clock -- Harold settles into his recliner and turns on whatever game is on. He loves the Tigers, follows them all season. I'll sit in my chair with my quilting or knitting, or sometimes I'll work on a crossword. We don't talk a lot during that time, but we're together in the room, and that's enough. Sometimes I'll look up and he'll be dozing, and I just let him be. Around eight-thirty I start getting ready for bed. We're early-to-bed, early-to-rise people. Always have been.

follow_ups.question: Tell me more about Patricia's visits. What do you two usually do together?
follow_ups.response: Patricia usually comes by Tuesday afternoons and Saturday mornings. On Tuesdays she'll help me with anything I need -- a ride to the doctor, picking up a prescription, that sort of thing. On Saturdays we sometimes bake together or she'll help me with a quilting project. She learned to quilt from me, though she doesn't have the patience for the really detailed patterns. We talk about the grandchildren, about what's going on in her life. She's a good daughter. Very dependable, very organized -- she takes after me that way.

transcript.question_text: Are there anyone else in your immediate family whom you have not mentioned? Who are they, and what is your relationship with them like?
transcript.response: Well, both my brothers have passed. My brother Walter died in 2010 and my brother Frank in 2016. I miss them. Harold has a sister still living down in Indiana, but we only see her at Christmas anymore. She's getting on in years too. Other than that, the people I mentioned -- Harold, the three kids, the grandchildren and great-grandchildren -- that's really my whole world.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about anyone else in your life we haven't discussed (like friends or romantic partners). Are there people outside of your family who are important to you?
transcript.response: Oh yes, my bridge group. There are four of us -- me, Dorothy, Helen, and Margaret. We've been playing together every Thursday afternoon for, goodness, going on twenty years now. We rotate houses, and whoever's hosting makes coffee and something sweet. It's not just the cards, though I do like to win -- don't tell them I said that. It's the company. We talk about everything. Dorothy is my closest friend outside the family. She lives three streets over and we walk together sometimes on the path behind the library. I also know the ladies at the church fellowship group, and the volunteers at the library reading program. There's a woman named Linda who runs the senior center finance volunteer group -- she and I work together on the budgets. We get along well because she's organized like me. And then there's my walking club. A group of us seniors meet twice a week on the trail behind the library, just a nice flat path. Nothing strenuous, but it keeps me moving.

follow_ups.question: Tell me more about your bridge games. What's the atmosphere like?
follow_ups.response: Competitive, in a friendly way. We keep score, and I keep a little notebook with our running tallies -- I've been tracking who wins for years. Margaret says I take it too seriously, but she's the one who gets upset when she loses a rubber. We play for about two hours, and there's always gossip mixed in with the cards. Helen brings the best lemon bars. It's really the highlight of my week, honestly. It gives me something to look forward to every Thursday.

follow_ups.question: How did you get involved with the library volunteer program?
follow_ups.response: That started about twelve years ago. The library was looking for volunteers to help with their children's reading hour, and I saw a flyer at the church. I like working with small groups, so I signed up. I help organize the materials, set up the room, and sometimes I read to the little ones. It's a very structured program, which suits me. I go on Wednesday mornings, every week. The librarians are lovely people, and it gets me out of the house on a regular schedule.

transcript.question_text: Now let's talk about your current neighborhood. Tell me all about the neighborhood and area in which you are living now.
transcript.response: Saline is a wonderful little city. It's in Washtenaw County, just south of Ann Arbor, so you've got the university nearby but you're still in a smaller, quieter place. Our neighborhood is residential, mostly single-family homes, nice yards. A lot of the people on our street have been here as long as we have, or close to it. There are some younger families moving in now, which is nice -- you hear children playing again. We're close to downtown, which is just a few blocks of shops and restaurants. There's the library, which I go to every week. The senior center is nearby too. We have the trail behind the library where I walk with my group. The churches are all within a short drive. It feels like a real community. You see people you know at the hardware store, at the post office. It's the kind of place where if you don't show up somewhere you're expected, someone will call and check on you.

follow_ups.question: How has the neighborhood changed over the years since you moved here in 1978?
follow_ups.response: When we first came, it was even smaller. The downtown had maybe half the shops it has now. There's been some growth, new subdivisions on the edges of town, and Ann Arbor has gotten bigger so Saline feels a little less separate than it used to. But the heart of it is the same. The library got an expansion, the senior center got updated, and they put in that nice walking trail. Those are all improvements, I think. What I don't love is the traffic -- it's picked up on Michigan Avenue. But compared to most places, we've got it good.

follow_ups.question: Do you interact much with the newer families who have moved onto your street?
follow_ups.response: A little. There's a young couple two doors down, and when they moved in I brought over a plate of cookies -- that's what you do. They're nice people, busy with their jobs. They don't sit on the porch the way we used to, but they wave and they keep their yard tidy, so we get along fine. I don't know everyone on the street the way I once did, but that's just how things are now. People are on their phones instead of chatting over the fence.

transcript.question_text: Some people say they feel really safe in their neighborhoods, others not so much. How about for you?
transcript.response: I feel very safe. I've never had a problem here. We don't even lock the garage most of the time, though Patricia says I should. The worst thing that's happened is somebody's dog getting into my garden a few years back and digging up my carrots. Saline has very little crime. You hear about things happening in bigger cities, and I'm grateful we're here. I do lock the front door at night, of course, and Harold keeps the porch light on. But I've never felt afraid in this neighborhood, not once in almost fifty years.

follow_ups.question: Has that sense of safety changed at all as you've gotten older?
follow_ups.response: Maybe a little. I'm more aware of my footing now -- I worry about slipping on ice in the winter more than I worry about anything else. And Harold and I try not to be out driving after dark because his eyes aren't what they used to be. But in terms of feeling safe from other people, no, that hasn't changed. This is a good, quiet neighborhood.

transcript.question_text: Living any place has its ups and downs. Tell me about what it's been like for you living here.
transcript.response: The ups far outweigh the downs. We raised our children here, we know the community, we're close to Robert and Patricia. The downs -- well, Michigan winters are hard, especially at our age. The cold gets into my joints, and shoveling is out of the question now. Robert comes by to clear the driveway, or we hire a neighborhood kid. And being in a smaller town means sometimes you have to drive to Ann Arbor for certain things -- a specialist doctor, a particular store. But honestly, for the life we want to live at this stage, Saline is just right. Everything I need is within a ten-minute drive.

follow_ups.question: Has there ever been a time you considered leaving Saline?
follow_ups.response: James suggested we move to Ohio to be near him when Harold had a rough patch a few years back. But I told him, 'This is our home.' I know every inch of this house. I know where the grocery store is, where the doctor's office is, where the church is. Starting over somewhere new at eighty-five? No, thank you. I'd rather stay here where things are familiar and I've got my people around me.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about the people who live with you right now, even people who are staying here temporarily.
transcript.response: It's just Harold and me, the two of us. It's been that way since Patricia moved out in 1992 -- she was the last one. The house feels bigger than it needs to be sometimes, especially now that it's just the two of us rattling around in three bedrooms. But we've got our routines. Harold has his side of the house -- his recliner, his garage workshop -- and I have mine: the kitchen, my sewing room where I keep my quilting frame and all my fabrics. We come together for meals, for the evening, for church on Sundays. It works. We know how to be around each other after sixty-four years.

follow_ups.question: Tell me about your sewing room. What does that space look like?
follow_ups.response: It's the spare bedroom upstairs. I've got my quilting frame set up by the window where the light is good. Shelves of fabric organized by color -- I'm particular about that. My yarn basket is in the corner for when I'm knitting. I've got patterns pinned to a corkboard, and there's a little radio that I play while I work. I spend a lot of time in there, especially in the winter when I can't be in the garden. I'm working on a quilt right now that has a log cabin pattern. It's for one of the great-grandchildren.

follow_ups.question: How do you and Harold divide the household responsibilities?
follow_ups.response: I handle the inside -- cooking, cleaning, laundry, bills, groceries. Harold handles the outside -- the yard, the gutters, snow removal, though he needs help with that now. He also does small repairs around the house. We've always divided it that way, and it works for us. I do the budget and pay all the bills. Harold trusts me with the money completely. He'll say, 'You're the numbers person,' and he's right.

transcript.question_text: Is anyone in your household a temporary member, or is everyone a permanent member of the household?
transcript.response: Everyone is permanent. It's just Harold and me, and we're not going anywhere.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about anyone else who stays here from time to time, if there is anyone like that
transcript.response: The grandchildren stay over sometimes at holidays -- we set up the spare room for them. And Patricia will stay the night if the weather's bad or if she's worried about us for some reason. But it's not regular.

transcript.question_text: Is there anyone who usually lives here but is away -- traveling, at school, or in a hospital?
transcript.response: No. It's just the two of us here, and we're both home.

transcript.question_text: Right now, across a typical week, how do your days vary?
transcript.response: My week is pretty structured. I like it that way. Every day starts the same -- I'm up by six, sometimes six-fifteen. I make coffee, have a bowl of oatmeal with a little cinnamon, and check my garden if the weather allows. Mondays I do laundry and general housework. Tuesdays Patricia comes by in the afternoon, and that's usually when we run errands or I have a doctor's appointment. Wednesdays I go to the library for the reading volunteer program in the morning. Thursdays are my bridge game at two o'clock -- that's sacred, don't schedule anything on a Thursday afternoon. Fridays I go to the senior center for the finance volunteer work, help with their books. Saturdays Patricia comes again, and sometimes we bake or I work on a quilt. Sundays we go to services in the morning, then have a bigger lunch at home, and Harold watches whatever game is on while I read or do a crossword. I also walk with my walking club on Tuesdays and Thursdays, early morning, on the trail behind the library.

follow_ups.question: You mentioned walking on Tuesdays and Thursdays. What time is that, and how long do you walk?
follow_ups.response: We meet at eight in the morning. There are usually six or seven of us, sometimes more if the weather's nice. We walk for about thirty to forty minutes -- nothing too fast, just a steady pace on the flat trail. It's about a mile and a half, maybe two. I like the regularity of it. My knees can be stiff in the morning, but once I get moving it helps.

follow_ups.question: What does your evening routine look like?
follow_ups.response: Supper is at five o'clock sharp. I usually cook something simple -- a casserole, a soup, or I'll warm up something I made earlier in the week. After we eat, Harold does the dishes -- that's been his job for decades -- and then we settle in for the evening. I'll quilt or knit, he'll watch TV. I might work on a crossword puzzle. By eight-thirty I'm getting ready for bed -- wash up, read a few pages of my book, lights out by nine. I sleep better when everything's on schedule.

transcript.question_text: At what kind of job or jobs do you work, and when do you work?
transcript.response: Well, I haven't worked for pay in a very long time. I stopped working outside the home when I had the children in the sixties, and I never went back to a paying job. What I do now is volunteer work, which I take just as seriously. I volunteer at the senior center managing their small fund -- keeping track of donations, expenses for events, making sure everything balances. I do that on Fridays, usually spend two or three hours there. And I volunteer at the library's reading program on Wednesday mornings, about two hours. I also organize the fellowship meetings at church -- that's more behind the scenes, planning the schedule, ordering supplies, coordinating who's bringing what dish. It's unpaid, but it keeps me busy and feeling useful. I treat it like a job -- I keep records, I'm on time, I follow through.

follow_ups.question: Tell me more about the senior center finance work. What does that look like day-to-day?
follow_ups.response: When I go in on Fridays, I sit down with the ledger -- yes, I still keep a paper ledger, though Linda has it on the computer too -- and I reconcile any donations that came in during the week, check receipts from supplies we bought, make sure the checking account lines up. If there's an event coming up, like a holiday luncheon, I'll put together a budget for it. I'm very careful with other people's money. Every penny has to be accounted for. Linda jokes that I'm the strictest auditor the senior center has ever had.

follow_ups.question: How long have you been volunteering at the senior center?
follow_ups.response: About eight years now. I started after I stepped back from some of my church duties -- I was doing too much at one point and it was wearing me out. The senior center needed someone who was good with numbers, and that's me. It gives me a purpose. At eighty-five, you need that. You need a reason to get up and get dressed and go somewhere.

transcript.question_text: Do you have other routines or responsibilities that you did not already share?
transcript.response: I do the bird-watching in the backyard. I've got three feeders set up, and every morning after I check the garden, I refill them and sit on the back porch for a little while to see who shows up. I keep a notebook of what birds I see -- cardinals, blue jays, chickadees, sparrows mostly. In the spring and fall I'll sometimes spot something unusual and I write that down too. I also spend time on my quilting most days, even if it's just twenty or thirty minutes. And I read -- I'm always in the middle of a historical fiction novel. Right now I'm reading one about women during the Civil War. I go through about two books a month. The library holds them for me at the desk because they know what I like.

follow_ups.question: What draws you to historical fiction specifically?
follow_ups.response: I like learning about how people lived in other times, but I want the story, not just the facts. A good historical novel makes you feel like you're there. I especially like stories about ordinary women, not queens or famous people, but regular women who kept households running during hard times. I think I see myself in those stories, in a way. My favorite recently was one set during the Depression about a Michigan farm family. That felt very close to home.

follow_ups.question: Tell me about your quilting projects. How do you decide what to make?
follow_ups.response: I usually make quilts as gifts -- for the grandchildren, for new babies in the family or at church, sometimes for the annual church fundraiser. I like traditional patterns: log cabin, nine-patch, double wedding ring. I plan each quilt out carefully on graph paper before I cut a single piece of fabric. I pick my colors to match who it's for. The one I'm making now for the great-grandchild is blues and yellows. It takes me several months to finish one, working a little bit each day. I find it very calming -- the repetition, the precision. Every stitch has to be just so.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about any recent changes to your daily routine.
transcript.response: The biggest change is that I've had to slow down a bit physically. A year or so ago I started having more trouble with my knees, especially going up and down the stairs. So now I try to limit how many times a day I go upstairs to my sewing room -- I'll bring my knitting downstairs so I can work in the living room without climbing up again. Harold and I also stopped driving at night. We used to go to evening events at the church, but now we stick to daytime. I've also started doing more of my grocery list by phone -- Patricia picks things up for me, or Robert will grab what we need on his way over. I don't go to the store as much as I used to. I miss that, honestly. I liked going aisle by aisle with my list. But it takes a lot out of me now, being on my feet that long.

follow_ups.question: How have you adjusted emotionally to having to slow down?
follow_ups.response: It's hard. I'm a person who likes to be in control of my own schedule, my own errands. Having to depend on other people for rides or shopping -- that's not easy for me. I don't like being a burden, even though Patricia says I'm not one. I try to focus on what I can still do: I can still cook, still quilt, still do the books at the senior center. As long as I can do those things, I'm alright. But I'll admit, some days I get frustrated when my body won't cooperate with what my mind wants to do.

follow_ups.question: Has Harold had to make similar adjustments?
follow_ups.response: Yes, Harold's slowed down too. He used to do all the yard work, but now Robert mows the lawn and Harold just tinkers with small things in the garage. His hearing isn't great -- I have to speak up or he misses half of what I say. And like I mentioned, he doesn't drive at night anymore either. But he's still steady, still here. We lean on each other. When my knees are bad, he heats up the leftovers. When his back acts up, I handle his share of the chores. That's what marriage is after sixty-four years.

transcript.question_text: If you have children, tell me about a typical weekday during the school year for your children. What is their daily routine? And what are after school activities your children participate in?
transcript.response: Well, my children are all grown now -- they're in their fifties and sixties. But back when they were school-age here in Saline, I ran a tight ship. The boys were up at six-thirty, breakfast by seven -- usually eggs and toast, nothing fancy but filling. They walked to school, which was close enough. Patricia took the bus later when she was in elementary school. After school, the boys did sports -- Robert was in baseball, James ran track. Patricia did her homework first, always, just like I taught her. Everyone was home by five-thirty for dinner. After dinner was homework time, then baths, then bed by nine. We didn't have a lot of extracurricular chaos -- I kept it manageable. I didn't want them over-scheduled. I believe children need routine and rest, and that's what they got.

follow_ups.question: Do you see any of those routines reflected in how your children raise their own kids now?
follow_ups.response: Patricia is the most like me. She keeps a schedule for her family, makes lists, plans ahead. Robert is more relaxed, but he still values family dinners, which makes me happy. James is his own man -- he does things differently, but his kids turned out fine, so who am I to judge. I do think the grandchildren have too many activities, running from one thing to the next. When they visit, I try to slow things down. We sit. We play cards. We bake cookies. They don't need to be entertained every minute.

transcript.question_text: Some people we've talked to tell us about experiences with law enforcement. How about for you?
transcript.response: I've been very fortunate -- I've had almost no run-ins with law enforcement. The most memorable thing is, oh, maybe fifteen years ago, a Saline police officer came by because there had been some break-ins a few neighborhoods over and they were going door to door reminding people to lock up. He was very polite, very professional. I offered him a glass of water and he declined but said thank you. Other than that, I got a speeding ticket once back in the eighties when I was in a hurry to pick up James from practice. Harold never let me forget that. But no, nothing serious. We're law-abiding people, and the local police here are good neighbors. You see them at the Fourth of July parade, at the school events. It's a small town.

follow_ups.question: How do you generally feel about the police in your community?
follow_ups.response: I think they do a good job. Saline is a safe place, and I think they help keep it that way. I know some people have different experiences, and I understand that. But for me and my family, the police have always been respectful. I appreciate that they check in on the neighborhood. A couple of times when Harold accidentally set off the smoke alarm cooking, they came by just to make sure everything was alright. That's reassuring at our age.

transcript.question_text: What other experiences with law enforcement stand out in your mind?
transcript.response: I really can't think of any others that stand out personally. The only other thing is, James had a fender-bender years ago, nothing serious, and the officer who responded was perfectly fair about it. Beyond that, law enforcement just hasn't been a big part of my life, and I'm grateful for that.

transcript.question_text: Some people tell us about experiences of arrest -- of loved ones, family members, friends, or themselves. How about for you?
transcript.response: No, nothing like that. Nobody in my immediate family has ever been arrested. I'm thankful for that. We've been a pretty quiet, by-the-book family. I know that's a blessing and not everyone has that experience, but that's been ours.

transcript.question_text: Some people say they vote in every election, some tell us they don't vote at all. How about you?
transcript.response: I vote in every election. Every single one -- federal, state, local, school board, you name it. I have not missed an election since I turned twenty-one, back before they lowered the voting age. My parents taught me that voting is a duty, not a choice. Harold and I go together to the polling place, first thing in the morning. I always research the candidates and the ballot measures ahead of time. I write out a little cheat sheet to take with me so I don't forget how I decided on the smaller races. I take it very seriously.

follow_ups.question: How do you research the candidates and issues before you vote?
follow_ups.response: I read the local newspaper -- the Saline Post -- and I pick up the voter guides that come in the mail. For the bigger elections, I'll watch some of the debates on TV, though I find a lot of them more shouting than substance these days. I talk things over with Harold and sometimes with Dorothy, though we don't always agree. I try to focus on what the candidates have actually done, not just what they say they'll do. I keep my notes on a notepad and go through them the night before the election.

follow_ups.question: Has voting always felt this important to you, or has it become more so over time?
follow_ups.response: It's always been important. My father used to say, 'If you don't vote, you don't get to complain.' I took that to heart. If anything, I feel more strongly about it now because I've seen so many elections and so many changes. I've voted for presidents who disappointed me and presidents who surprised me. But I always show up. That's the deal.

transcript.question_text: How would you describe your political views?
transcript.response: I'd say I'm someone who thinks about each issue on its own rather than following a party line completely. I grew up in a house where you worked hard, you saved your money, you didn't expect handouts, and you took care of your community. That shapes how I see a lot of things. I believe in being careful with how the government spends money -- I run a tight budget at home and I expect the same from the people in charge. I don't like waste. At the same time, I think there are programs that help people who genuinely need it, and I support that. The senior center where I volunteer, for example, gets some public funding and it does real good in this community. I get uncomfortable with a lot of the anger in politics lately. I read the local paper and I watch the evening news, and it just seems like everyone is yelling at each other. I miss the days when people could disagree without being hateful about it. On social issues, I tend to think that people should be allowed to live their lives, but I also hold on to some of the values I was raised with. I don't always fit neatly into one box, and I'm okay with that.

follow_ups.question: Are there particular issues that matter most to you when you're deciding how to vote?
follow_ups.response: At this stage of my life, I pay close attention to anything that affects people my age -- how the government handles retirement benefits, how they fund programs for seniors, what they're doing about the cost of the pills I take every day. Property taxes too, since we own our home and we're on a fixed income. I also care about the local school board because I want good schools for the grandchildren and for the community. And I want roads that aren't falling apart -- that's a real issue in Michigan.

follow_ups.question: You mentioned you read the local paper and watch the evening news. Which ones?
follow_ups.response: I get the Saline Post delivered, and I read the Ann Arbor News when I can get a copy at the library. For TV, I watch the evening news at six o'clock -- usually the broadcast network, whichever one Harold has on. I used to watch more cable news but I stopped because it was making me upset. Too much arguing. Now I stick to the regular news and the newspaper. I also listen to the local radio sometimes in the morning while I'm in the kitchen.

follow_ups.question: How has the tone of political conversation changed from when you were younger?
follow_ups.response: It's like night and day. When I was younger, people disagreed, sure, but there was a basic respect. At church, at the dinner table, you could talk about the president and not start a fight. Now it seems like politics has gotten personal in a way it never used to be. People don't just disagree, they stop talking to each other over it. I've seen it at the bridge table -- we had to make a rule, no politics during cards, because it was ruining the game. That makes me sad. I think you can hold your views and still be civil.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about any recent changes in your political views.
transcript.response: I wouldn't say my core views have changed much, but what's changed is how I feel about the whole system. I'm more discouraged than I used to be. It seems like nothing gets done because everyone's fighting. The cost of things keeps going up -- groceries, medications, property taxes -- and nobody in office seems to be doing anything about it. I've also become more aware that the world my grandchildren are growing up in is very different from the one I knew. Some of the issues they talk about, like the environment and technology and how people communicate, those weren't on my radar before. I'm trying to listen more, even when I don't fully understand. My granddaughter has opened my eyes to some things about how young people see the world.

follow_ups.question: What kinds of things has your granddaughter opened your eyes to?
follow_ups.response: She talks about the cost of college, for one thing. She has student loans that are more than what we paid for our house, which is just unbelievable to me. And she worries about the environment in a way that my generation didn't really think about. She recycles everything, tries to buy from local businesses. I've started paying more attention to that -- I try to bring my own bags to the store now, things like that. I may be eighty-five, but I can still learn.

transcript.question_text: One topic a lot of people have been talking about recently is race and/or racism and policing. Some tell us issues raised by the Black Lives Matter movement have affected them a lot, some say they've affected them somewhat, others say they haven't affected them at all. How about for you?
transcript.response: I'd say it's affected me somewhat, in the sense that I've been paying attention to it and thinking about it. I grew up in a town that was almost entirely white, and Saline is mostly white too, so I'll be honest and say that my day-to-day life doesn't bring me into contact with a lot of diversity. But I watch the news, and I see the stories, and some of them are very disturbing. The videos of people being hurt by police -- that's hard to watch regardless of who you are. I think everyone deserves to be treated fairly and with respect. My upbringing taught me that you treat people the way you want to be treated, and I still believe that. But I also know that my experience with police has been nothing but positive, so it's hard for me to fully understand what other people go through.

follow_ups.question: Has this topic come up in conversations with your family or friends?
follow_ups.response: It has, mostly with the younger generation. My grandchildren feel very strongly about these issues. They've talked to me about it, and I listen. I don't always know what to say, because I grew up in a different time and a different place. But I try to hear them out. At bridge, we agreed not to discuss it because it got too heated. Dorothy and I talked about it privately once or twice, and we both agreed it's sad that people are treated differently because of how they look. Beyond that, I mostly keep my thoughts to myself on this one.

transcript.question_text: How have you been thinking about the issues Black Lives Matter raises?
transcript.response: I think the basic idea -- that every person's life matters and that nobody should be mistreated -- is something I agree with completely. That's just being a decent human being. Where it gets complicated for me is when the conversation turns to big policy changes or when protests become destructive. I'm someone who believes in order and working within the system to make changes. I vote, I write the occasional letter to my representative, and I think that's the right way to do it. But I also understand that if the system hasn't been working for certain people, they might feel like they have to do something bigger to be heard. I'm still working through all of it, honestly. I'm an eighty-five-year-old white woman from a small Michigan town -- I know there's a lot I don't fully understand about other people's experiences.

follow_ups.question: You said you sometimes write to your representative. Have you done that recently about any of these issues?
follow_ups.response: Not specifically about policing, no. The last letters I wrote were about prescription costs and road funding -- things that directly affect me. But I did sign a card at church once that was a general statement about treating all people with dignity. That felt right to me. I'm not one for big public statements, but I try to live by my values in my own small way.

transcript.question_text: How have you been thinking about race in the U.S. recently?
transcript.response: I've been thinking that it's more complicated than I ever realized when I was younger. Growing up, race just wasn't something we talked about in my family or my community. It wasn't that we were hostile -- it was that we were sheltered. Everyone around us looked the same and had similar backgrounds. It wasn't until I started watching the news more closely and talking to my grandchildren that I began to understand how different other people's experiences are. I think this country has come a long way since I was young, but clearly there's still a long way to go. I want my great-grandchildren to grow up in a world where it doesn't matter what you look like. Whether we get there, I don't know, but I hope so.

follow_ups.question: Has your thinking about race evolved over the decades?
follow_ups.response: Yes, I think so. When I was young, the civil rights movement was happening, and I remember my parents talking about it at the dinner table. My father was a quiet man, but he said everyone deserves a fair shake. That stuck with me. Over the years, I've met people through the church and through volunteering who come from different backgrounds, and that's broadened my perspective. I still have a lot to learn, but I'm not the same person I was at twenty or even at sixty.

transcript.question_text: How have you responded to the increased focus on race and/or racism and policing? Some people tell us they're aware of the issue but keep their thoughts to themselves, others say they've talked to family and friends, others have joined protests. What about you?
transcript.response: I'm mostly in the camp of keeping my thoughts to myself and listening. I've had some conversations with my grandchildren, and I talked to Dorothy about it a couple of times. But I haven't joined any protests or done anything public. At my age and in my community, I think the best thing I can do is treat everyone I meet with kindness and respect, and try to understand perspectives different from my own. That might sound small, but it's genuine.

transcript.question_text: What about people you're close to? How have they responded to the increased focus on race and/or racism and policing?
transcript.response: My grandchildren are the most vocal. A couple of them have been to marches and they post things on the internet about it. They feel very strongly, and I respect that even when I don't fully follow everything they're talking about. My children are more like me -- they're aware, they care, but they're not out marching. Patricia and I have talked about it, and she said she's been trying to learn more, reading books and such. Harold doesn't say much about it, to be honest. He watches the news and shakes his head, but he's not one for big discussions. At the bridge table and at church, people mostly avoid the topic. There's a feeling that it's too charged, too likely to cause a rift. So we stick to safer subjects.

follow_ups.question: How do you feel about the younger generation being more vocal about these issues?
follow_ups.response: I think it's their right and maybe their responsibility. They're going to inherit this world, and they should speak up about what they want it to look like. I didn't do that at their age -- I was focused on my family and my home. But times are different now. I just hope they channel their passion into things that actually make a difference, like voting and community work, not just arguing on the internet.

transcript.question_text: Now we'd like to learn more about your health. First, tell me all about your health.
transcript.response: Well, at eighty-five, you've got to expect some wear and tear. My knees are the biggest issue -- going up stairs, getting out of a chair, that sort of thing. They're stiff in the morning and worse when it's cold. I take a couple of pills for that every day. I also take something to keep my numbers in a good range -- I go in every few months and they check my levels, make sure the ticker is doing what it should. The doctor has me on a low-salt diet, which means I've had to change how I cook some of my recipes. I used to salt everything, but now I use more herbs from the garden instead. My eyesight isn't what it was, so I use a magnifying glass for the fine stitching on my quilts. And my hearing -- well, Harold's hearing is worse than mine, so we're both turning the TV up louder than we should. But overall, for eighty-five, I'm managing. I can still get around, still think clearly, still take care of most things myself.

follow_ups.question: You mentioned pills for your knees and to keep your numbers right. Can you walk me through your daily pill routine?
follow_ups.response: I have a pill organizer -- one of those weekly ones with the little compartments. I fill it every Sunday night for the whole week. In the morning with breakfast, I take the ones for my joints and the one the doctor says is for my levels. Then in the evening I have one more small pill. Patricia helped me set up a chart on the refrigerator so I never forget. I'm very particular about taking them at the same time every day. The doctor seems pleased with how I'm doing, so whatever's in those pills must be working.

follow_ups.question: How has switching to a low-salt diet affected your cooking?
follow_ups.response: It was an adjustment, I'll tell you. My mother salted everything -- the sauerkraut, the pot roast, the soup. When the doctor told me to cut back, I thought, 'Well, everything's going to taste like cardboard.' But I started using more rosemary, thyme, garlic, and a little lemon juice, and honestly, some things taste better now. The chicken pot pie, for instance -- I use the herbs from my garden and it's got more flavor than it ever did. Harold didn't like it at first, but he's used to it now. I still keep a little salt shaker on the table, but I barely touch it.

transcript.question_text: For you, what makes it easy or hard to stay healthy?
transcript.response: What makes it easier is having a routine. I eat the same breakfast every day, I walk with my group twice a week, I go to bed at the same time. That regularity keeps me on track. Having Patricia nearby to help with doctor's appointments is a big help too. What makes it hard is the cold. Michigan winters are brutal on my joints, and there are days when I just don't want to get out of bed because everything aches. Also, getting to the doctor's office is harder now that I don't drive as much. I depend on Patricia or Robert for rides, and I don't like depending on people. And honestly, the cost of those pills has gone up, and that weighs on me even though we manage it.

follow_ups.question: What do you do on those cold winter days when getting moving is especially difficult?
follow_ups.response: I force myself out of bed at the same time regardless. That's the discipline in me. I'll take a warm shower to loosen up, and I do some gentle stretches that the doctor showed me -- just standing at the counter, bending my knees a little, rolling my ankles. Then I have my oatmeal and coffee and try to get going. If it's too icy to walk outside, I'll walk back and forth inside the house for a bit just to get the blood moving. I refuse to turn into someone who just sits in a chair all day.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about anything big that has happened in the past two years related to your health: any medical diagnoses, flare-ups of chronic conditions, broken bones, pain -- anything like that.
transcript.response: About eighteen months ago, my knees got bad enough that the doctor changed my pills to something stronger. I was having a lot of trouble going up the stairs, and some mornings I could barely get out of bed without holding onto something. He talked about maybe doing a procedure on the knees eventually, but I told him I'd rather manage it with pills and exercises for now. I don't want to be in a hospital if I can avoid it. Other than that, I had a bad cold last winter that turned into something in my chest, and I was down for about three weeks. Patricia came and stayed with us for a few days during the worst of it. That scared me, honestly -- at my age, a bad chest cold can turn into something serious. The doctor put me on a round of medicine, and I cleared up, but it took a while to get my energy back.

follow_ups.question: How did that chest illness affect your daily routine while you were recovering?
follow_ups.response: Everything stopped. I couldn't go to the library, couldn't make it to bridge, couldn't even stand in the kitchen long enough to cook. Harold heated up soup and made sandwiches, bless him. I missed three weeks of my volunteer work and it drove me crazy. I kept a list of everything I needed to catch up on when I got better. The walking group went on without me, and Dorothy called every day to check in. Getting back to my routine afterward was like slowly turning the engine back on. I started with short walks around the house, then back to the trail, then back to my regular schedule. It took about a month to feel fully like myself.

follow_ups.question: Has that experience changed how you think about your health going forward?
follow_ups.response: It has. I'm more careful now. I wash my hands constantly, I try to stay away from people who are sniffling, and I make sure I'm getting enough rest. I also started taking an extra supplement that the doctor suggested to keep my defenses up. I don't take any chances anymore. At eighty-five, you can't just bounce back the way you used to.

transcript.question_text: Sometimes, health problems get in the way. They can even affect people's ability to work or care for their children. How about you?
transcript.response: Well, my children are long grown, so child care isn't the issue. But my health does affect my volunteer work. When my knees are bad or I'm fighting a cold, I can't get to the senior center or the library. I hate missing my commitments. And at home, there are things I can't do anymore -- I can't scrub the floors on my hands and knees, I can't carry heavy bags of groceries, I can't garden for hours the way I used to. I've had to accept help, which doesn't come naturally to me. But I'd rather accept help and keep doing the things I love in a limited way than give up on them entirely.

follow_ups.question: How do you decide when to push through and when to rest?
follow_ups.response: I listen to my body more than I used to. If my knees are really swollen, I know that's a day to stay downstairs and knit instead of going up to the sewing room. If I'm exhausted after walking, I'll sit on the porch and watch the birds instead of starting a chore. It's a negotiation, every day. I push when I can and rest when I must. Harold's good at telling me when I'm overdoing it -- he'll say, 'Sit down, Deeva,' and I know he means it.

transcript.question_text: Sometimes, it's not your health problem, but the health of a loved one. Has this been an issue for you?
transcript.response: Yes. Harold had a scare about five years ago where he was getting dizzy and short of breath, and they found something going on with his heart. He had to go through a procedure and then take new pills, and for a while he was very tired and not himself. That was the most frightened I've been in a long time. I kept thinking, what would I do without him? I managed his pills, made sure he went to every follow-up appointment, kept track of what the doctors said in a little notebook. He's stable now, but I still watch him closely. If he looks pale or breathes heavy, I'm on it. And my brother Frank, before he passed, he was in a care facility for two years. I visited him every week until the end. That was draining -- watching someone you love decline like that. It reminded me that health is everything.

follow_ups.question: How did Harold's health scare affect your own well-being during that time?
follow_ups.response: I didn't sleep well for months. I'd lie there listening to make sure he was breathing. I lost weight because I wasn't eating properly -- I was so focused on taking care of him that I forgot about myself. Dorothy noticed and brought me meals a few times. Eventually, once Harold was stable, I got back on track. But that period showed me how much I rely on him, even though I think of myself as independent. We're a team, and when one of us is down, the whole system wobbles.

follow_ups.question: Do you worry about the possibility of one of you needing long-term care?
follow_ups.response: I think about it, yes. Seeing what happened with Frank, the cost of that facility, the quality of the care -- it worries me. Harold and I have talked about it a little. We both want to stay in this house as long as possible. Patricia has said she'll be there for us, and Robert too. But I know there may come a day when we need more help than the family can give. We've set some money aside for that, but whether it's enough, I honestly don't know.

transcript.question_text: Tell me what it has been like trying to get the health care you or your immediate family need. Have you ever had to forgo getting the health care you need?
transcript.response: We've been fortunate in that we have our government health coverage for seniors, so the basics are covered. Doctor visits, hospital stays, most of the pills. But there are gaps. Some of the medications have a portion I pay out of pocket, and that adds up. There was one pill the doctor wanted me to try for my knees that would have been over a hundred dollars a month after what the coverage pays, and I said no, let's stick with the cheaper one. Is that forgoing care? I don't know. It felt like a budget decision. Getting to appointments is the other challenge. The doctors I need are in Ann Arbor, which is a twenty-minute drive. I can't always go on my own, so I have to coordinate with Patricia or Robert. I've postponed appointments before because I didn't want to bother them, and Patricia got upset with me about that. She said, 'Mom, your health comes first.' She's right, but old habits die hard.

follow_ups.question: How do you manage the out-of-pocket costs for your medications?
follow_ups.response: I budget for it. Every month I know exactly how much the pills cost, and I set that money aside first, before anything else. It's about sixty to seventy dollars a month between the two of us after the coverage kicks in. There's a program at the pharmacy that gives a small discount for seniors, and I make sure we use that. I also compare prices sometimes -- Patricia showed me how to look it up on the computer. It's not luxurious, but we manage.

transcript.question_text: Have you or your immediate family ever used alternative forms of medicine? This might include indigenous, non-western, or informal forms of care.
transcript.response: Not in any formal way, but I do use home remedies that my mother taught me. If I have a sore throat, I gargle with warm salt water. If I can't sleep, I drink chamomile tea. I use peppermint oil on my temples when I get a headache. My mother always had a jar of honey with lemon in the pantry for colds. I still make that every fall. I also believe strongly in the garden as medicine -- not in a mystical way, but in the sense that getting your hands in the dirt, being outside, eating fresh vegetables, that all keeps you healthier. Harold swears by a tablespoon of apple cider vinegar every morning, though I think that's more habit than medicine. We haven't done anything like acupuncture or chiropractic -- I'm a bit skeptical of things I don't understand. But the home remedies, those I trust because they came from my mother.

follow_ups.question: Do you grow any of those remedies in your garden?
follow_ups.response: I grow chamomile for tea, peppermint for the oil and for cooking, and I always have lemons on hand though those I buy. The rosemary and thyme aren't just for cooking -- rosemary is supposed to be good for circulation, and thyme is good for coughs. I don't know if the science backs all of that up, but it's what I was taught, and it hasn't steered me wrong yet.

transcript.question_text: During tough times, some people tell us they cope by smoking or drinking. How about for you?
transcript.response: I've never smoked, not once. My father smoked, and I hated the smell of it. When he passed, it was partly because of what all those years of smoking did to his lungs, and that was enough to keep me away forever. As for drinking, I'll have a small glass of wine at holidays -- Christmas, Thanksgiving, a birthday toast. But that's it. Maybe five or six times a year. Harold used to have a beer on weekends when he was younger, but he barely drinks now either. We're not teetotalers, but alcohol has never been something either of us leaned on during hard times. When I'm stressed, I quilt. I clean the house. I organize something. That's my coping. Keeping my hands busy and my surroundings in order makes me feel like I'm in control, even when things aren't going well.

follow_ups.question: When was the last time you felt that kind of stress and turned to those habits?
follow_ups.response: During Harold's heart scare. I cleaned this house from top to bottom while he was in the hospital. Patricia came by and found me scrubbing the kitchen cabinets at ten o'clock at night. She said, 'Mom, what are you doing?' And I said, 'If I stop moving, I'll start worrying.' That's how I'm wired. If I keep my hands busy, my mind stays calmer.

transcript.question_text: Other people say they cope by relying on prescriptions, pain medications, marijuana, or other substances. How about for you and can you describe your most recent experience of using them, if any?
transcript.response: The only prescriptions I use are the ones the doctor gave me for my knees and my levels, and I take those exactly as directed -- no more, no less. I've never used marijuana or anything like that. When my knees are really bad, sometimes I'll take an extra over-the-counter pain reliever, but I always check with the doctor first. I'm very careful about medications. I keep a list of everything I take in my wallet so that if I ever end up in the emergency room, the doctors know exactly what's in my system. I'm not comfortable with the idea of relying on pills beyond what's necessary. My mother's generation got by with aspirin and hot water bottles, and I try to keep that same mindset.

follow_ups.question: Do you ever worry about the number of pills you take as you've gotten older?
follow_ups.response: I do. When I was sixty, I took nothing. Now I have a whole row of bottles in the cabinet. Every time the doctor adds something, I ask, 'Is this really necessary?' I don't want to be one of those people rattling around with a bag full of pills. But so far everything I take has a clear purpose, and I can see the difference when I take them versus when I forget. So I accept it as part of being eighty-five.

transcript.question_text: How are you and your family coping with, or paying for, your health care needs right now?
transcript.response: Our government health coverage for seniors covers the bulk of it, which is a blessing. Doctor visits, hospital stays, the main prescriptions -- those are mostly covered. We pay out of pocket for the portions that aren't covered, which runs about sixty to seventy dollars a month. Harold's heart medications are the biggest chunk. I budget for it just like any other bill -- it goes in the ledger, first of the month. The kids help when something unexpected comes up. When Harold needed that procedure, there were some costs that weren't fully covered, and Robert and Patricia split it between them without us asking. I didn't like taking the money, but they insisted. We also have a small emergency fund that I've been adding to for years, a little bit each month. It's not much, but it's there for when we need it.

follow_ups.question: How do you feel about relying on your children for some of those costs?
follow_ups.response: Grateful but uncomfortable. I raised them to be independent, and I'd rather be the one helping them than the other way around. But I also know that at our age, this is just how it goes. They want to help, and I've learned to let them. Harold is better at accepting it than I am. He says, 'That's what family is for,' and he's probably right.

transcript.question_text: Some people are excited about medical vaccination, and others, not so much. How about you?
transcript.response: I'm a believer in them. I get my flu shot every year, and I got all the shots they recommended during the pandemic -- every round. At my age, I'm not taking chances. I grew up in a time when polio was still a real fear, and I remember the relief when that shot came along. My parents made sure all of us children got every shot available. I don't understand the people who refuse them. My doctor recommends them, and I trust my doctor. It's that simple for me.

follow_ups.question: Has anyone in your family or circle been hesitant about vaccines?
follow_ups.response: One of James's sons was on the fence during the pandemic, and I called him up and told him, 'Your grandmother didn't survive eighty-something years to be taken out by something preventable because you're reading nonsense on the internet.' He got his shots after that. I don't know if it was because of me or not, but I like to think I helped.

transcript.question_text: What are your trusted sources of information about the vaccine?
transcript.response: My doctor, first and foremost. If Dr. Hendricks tells me to get a shot, I get it. I also listen to what they say on the evening news -- the medical correspondents who explain things in plain language. And the pharmacy -- our pharmacist, Carol at the Rite Aid, she's been very helpful in explaining what's in the shots and what to expect. I don't go on the internet for medical information because I can't tell what's reliable and what's not. Patricia sometimes looks things up for me if I have a question, but she knows I'm going to listen to the doctor over anything else.

follow_ups.question: How long have you been going to Dr. Hendricks?
follow_ups.response: About fifteen years now. He's the one who's been managing my knees and my levels and all of it. He's patient, he explains things clearly, and he doesn't rush me. That matters when you're older -- some doctors talk to you like you can't understand, but he treats me like an adult. I trust him completely.

transcript.question_text: Now we're going to talk a bit more about what life was like for you over the past year. Tell me all about how you have been feeling.
transcript.response: Overall, I'd say I've been doing alright, but it's been a mixed bag. There are days when I feel grateful and purposeful -- I've got my volunteer work, my quilting, my family nearby. Those are good days. But there are other days when the aches and the limitations get to me, and I feel frustrated and a little down. Losing my brother Frank a few years back hit me hard, and even now I'll have moments where I think of him and feel a wave of sadness. And there are times when I look around and realize how many people from my generation are gone -- friends, family, people I grew up with. That loneliness of being one of the last ones standing, it's real. Harold and I have each other, and that's a tremendous comfort. But some mornings I wake up and everything hurts, and the house is quiet, and I have to give myself a little talking-to: 'Get up, Deeva. You have things to do.' And I do. I get up. But it's not always easy.

follow_ups.question: What do you do on those harder mornings to get yourself going?
follow_ups.response: I stick to the routine. That's my anchor. I put my feet on the floor, I go downstairs, I make the coffee. Once I'm moving, it gets easier. If it's a walking day, getting out to the trail with the group really helps -- being around other people, moving my body, breathing the fresh air. And I think about what's on the calendar for that day. If I have bridge or a volunteer shift, that gives me something to look forward to. The days that are hardest are the ones with nothing scheduled, which is why I try not to have too many of those.

follow_ups.question: Do you talk to anyone about how you're feeling on those difficult days?
follow_ups.response: Not much. I'm from a generation that doesn't really do that. Harold knows when I'm having a bad day -- he can tell by how quiet I am -- but we don't sit down and have big conversations about feelings. Dorothy is the one friend I might say something to, like, 'I'm having a rough week,' and she'll just nod and pour me another cup of coffee. That's enough. Patricia has tried to get me to talk more about it, and I appreciate that, but I'm more of a keep-it-inside-and-keep-moving type.

transcript.question_text: Tell me a story about a time in the last year when you were in a rough place or struggling emotionally.
transcript.response: Last winter, during that bad cold I mentioned, I was laid up for three weeks and I couldn't do any of my usual activities. I missed bridge for three weeks in a row, couldn't volunteer, couldn't garden because it was winter anyway. I was stuck in the house, coughing, weak, and dependent on Harold and Patricia for everything. About the second week, I was sitting in my chair, looking out the window at the snow, and I just started crying. Not sobbing, just quiet tears. I felt so useless. I thought, 'Is this what the rest of my life is going to be? Sitting in a chair, waiting for someone to bring me soup?' That scared me more than the illness itself. But then I got better, slowly, and I got back to my routine, and the feeling passed. But I remember it. It's a reminder of what's at stake when my health goes sideways.

follow_ups.question: Did that experience change anything about how you approach your days now?
follow_ups.response: It made me more determined to stay active and keep my routine going. I don't take a single bridge game or a single walk for granted anymore. Every time I can get to the library on Wednesday or sit down with Dorothy and the cards on Thursday, I think, 'This is good. This is what keeps me going.' I also started being more careful about resting before I'm exhausted, so I don't run myself down and end up sick again.

follow_ups.question: Did you talk to Patricia or anyone about those tears?
follow_ups.response: No. I wiped my face and put on the television. Patricia was there making lunch and I didn't want her to see me like that. She worries enough already. I don't want to be a burden in any way -- physically or emotionally. I know that probably sounds stubborn, but it's how I was raised. You handle your own feelings and you move on.

transcript.question_text: Some people say they struggle with depression, anxiety, or something else like that. How about for you?
transcript.response: I wouldn't use those big words for what I experience, but I'll say this: there are weeks where everything feels heavier than it should. The mornings are harder to face, the chores feel like mountains, and I don't look forward to things the way I normally do. It comes and goes, usually tied to the weather or my health or something happening with Harold. Winter is the worst -- the short days, the cold, being stuck inside. I deal with it by keeping busy. If I sit still too long, my mind goes to dark places -- worrying about Harold, worrying about my own health, thinking about everyone I've lost. So I keep my hands moving. Quilting, knitting, crosswords, organizing something. As long as I'm doing something productive, I can keep those feelings at arm's length. I've never talked to a doctor about it or taken anything for it. I just push through.

follow_ups.question: You mentioned your mind going to dark places. Can you say more about what that looks like for you?
follow_ups.response: It's the worrying, mostly. I'll lie in bed at night running through every possible bad thing that could happen. What if Harold has another episode with his heart? What if I fall and break a hip? What if we run out of money for the medications? I'll go through these scenarios over and over, and by the time I stop, it's midnight and I'm wide awake. I've learned to combat it by getting up and making a list -- writing down what I'm worried about and what I can actually do about it. Once it's on paper, it feels more manageable. But those nighttime spirals, they happen more than I'd like to admit.

follow_ups.question: Has anyone ever suggested you talk to a professional about those feelings?
follow_ups.response: Patricia has mentioned it once or twice. She said her friend sees a counselor and it helps. But I told her, 'I'm eighty-five years old. I've been managing my own feelings my whole life.' I'm not opposed to it in principle -- I think it's fine for people who need it. But for me, I have my routine, my activities, and my faith. Those are my counselors, so to speak. If it ever got to the point where I couldn't function, that would be different. But so far, I manage.

transcript.question_text: How has it been for your family?
transcript.response: Patricia carries a lot of worry, I know that. She worries about Harold and me, about her own kids, about work. She doesn't always say it, but I can see it in her face when she comes by. Robert keeps things close to the chest, like his father. James, I'm not sure -- he's far enough away that I don't see the day-to-day. Harold is steady, like always, but I know the limitations of aging weigh on him. He doesn't say much, but he gets quiet when something's bothering him. He used to be the one who fixed everything, and now he can't, and I think that's hard for a man like him. We're all coping in our own ways, getting by, supporting each other as best we can.

transcript.question_text: Some people say that religion or spirituality is important in their lives, for others not so much. How about you?
transcript.response: It's been a part of my life since I can remember. My family went to services every Sunday when I was growing up, and Harold and I have done the same our whole marriage. We go to a church here in Saline -- been going there since we moved in 1978. I know everyone there, and they know me. I organize the fellowship meetings, help with the potlucks, used to manage the women's group treasury. It's not just the services, though -- it's the community. The people at that church have been there for us through everything: when the kids were born, when my parents passed, when Harold was in the hospital. They brought meals, they sent cards, they showed up. That's what it means to me -- it's less about the theology and more about belonging to something bigger than yourself. I do pray, mostly in the evening before bed. I ask for the health of my family, for guidance when I'm worried, and I give thanks for the good days. It gives me comfort, especially when things are hard. The choir at church is beautiful -- they do these old hymns with a piano, and some Sundays it's the most peaceful I feel all week.

follow_ups.question: Tell me about organizing the fellowship meetings. What does that involve?
follow_ups.response: The fellowship meetings happen twice a month, usually after the Sunday service. I plan the schedule -- who's speaking, what the topic is, what we'll serve. I keep a binder with everything: contact lists, sign-up sheets for who's bringing what dish, the budget for supplies. I order the paper plates and napkins, make sure the coffee is set up. It's a small group, maybe twenty people, but I want it to run smoothly. When people say, 'Deeva, that was a lovely fellowship,' that means the world to me. It's my way of serving the community.

follow_ups.question: Has your relationship with your faith changed over the years?
follow_ups.response: It's deepened, if anything. When I was young, going to services was just what you did -- you didn't question it. As I've gotten older and lost people I love, the comfort of it has become more important. I don't have all the answers about what happens after this life, but I find peace in the prayers and the hymns and the community. When Harold was in the hospital, I sat in our pew alone on Sunday morning and that was the hardest thing -- but also the most comforting, because the people around me understood and held me up without me having to ask.

follow_ups.question: Do you do anything spiritual outside of church?
follow_ups.response: My evening prayers, like I mentioned. And sometimes when I'm in the garden, I feel a sense of gratitude that I'd call spiritual. Watching the seeds come up, the birds at the feeder, the seasons changing -- it reminds me that there's an order to things that's bigger than my little worries. I also read the devotional booklet that the church provides. I keep it on my nightstand and read a passage before bed. It's a small thing, but it centers me.

transcript.question_text: Some people tell us they use Facebook, Instagram, or other social media to stay connected with the wider world. How about for you? Tell me all about how you use these types of platforms.
transcript.response: I do have a Facebook account, but Patricia set it up for me and I'm not what you'd call a heavy user. I go on maybe once or twice a week, usually on the computer at my desk. I look at photos the grandchildren post -- birthdays, vacations, the babies. Sometimes I'll click the little thumbs-up button. I've never posted anything myself. I don't really understand all the features, and I'm afraid I'll click something wrong and share my whole life with strangers. Patricia showed me how to use the video call feature on the computer, and I do that with James in Ohio every couple of weeks. That's wonderful -- being able to see his face while we talk. But Instagram, Twitter, all those other things -- no. I don't have the patience or the interest. I'd rather write a letter or pick up the phone. I think people spend too much time staring at their screens, honestly. But I'm glad the technology exists for staying in touch with the family members who are far away.

follow_ups.question: Have you ever found anything useful or enjoyable on Facebook, besides family photos?
follow_ups.response: There's a quilting group that Patricia added me to, and I do scroll through that sometimes. People post pictures of their quilts and patterns, and I've gotten a couple of ideas from it. But I don't comment or participate much. And there's the Saline community page where people post about local events. I've seen notices about library programs and senior center activities there. So it's not completely useless to me, but I could live without it.
