follow_ups.question: How do you feel about the amount of time younger people spend on social media?
follow_ups.response: I worry about it. The grandchildren are on their phones constantly. When they come to visit, I have to say, 'Put that thing away, we're having dinner.' I think they miss out on real conversations, real connections. I know that sounds old-fashioned, but when I sit down with Dorothy for bridge, we're looking at each other, talking, laughing. That's connection. I'm not sure tapping a screen all day gives you the same thing.

transcript.question_text: Some people say they use Facebook or Twitter to get (emotional or financial) support during tough times. What about you?
transcript.response: No, I would never do that. If I need support, I call Patricia or Dorothy. The idea of putting my troubles out there for everyone to see -- no. That's private. When Harold was in the hospital, a few people from church posted well-wishes on my Facebook page, and that was kind of them, but I responded by phone, not online. I just don't think the internet is the place for personal struggles. I know younger people feel differently, and that's their business. But for me, support comes from the people sitting across from you, not from strangers on a screen.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about any recent changes in your level of stress, worry, and your emotional coping strategies.
transcript.response: I'd say my worry level has gone up a bit in the last year or two, mostly because of the physical changes. Every new ache or pain makes me think, 'Is this something serious?' The cost of living going up has stressed me out too -- groceries are more expensive, the medications cost more, property taxes went up. I cope the same way I always have: I keep to my routine, I stay busy with my hands, I organize and plan so I feel in control. I've also started relying on Patricia more, which is both a comfort and a stress in itself, because I don't want to be a burden. One thing that has helped is that I've been doing more of my crosswords and reading -- those quiet activities calm my mind. And the walking has been good for my mood. The mornings I walk with the group, I always feel better for the rest of the day.

transcript.question_text: This might be repetitive, but before we go on to the next section, I want to quickly make sure I have this right: who are you living with right now?
transcript.response: Just my husband Harold. The two of us, in our house in Saline. That's it.

transcript.question_text: Any babies or small children?
transcript.response: No. The great-grandchildren visit, but they don't live here.

transcript.question_text: Anyone who usually lives here but is away -- traveling, at school, or in a hospital?
transcript.response: No, no one.

transcript.question_text: Any lodgers, boarders, or employees who live here?
transcript.response: No, nothing like that.

transcript.question_text: Who shares the responsibility for the rent, mortgage, or other household expenses?
transcript.response: Harold and I share it, though I manage the bills. The mortgage has been paid off for years.

transcript.question_text: Now we'd like to talk about how you make ends meet and what the monthly budget looks like for you and your family. What were your biggest expenses last month?
transcript.response: Last month the biggest expenses were property taxes -- we pay quarterly and it was due -- groceries, and Harold's medications. The property tax payment was the one that stung, because it went up again this year. After that, groceries are always near the top of the list. We eat at home almost exclusively, so the grocery bill is real. Utilities were moderate -- it's still cold in March so the heating bill was up. And then the medications, like I said, about sixty to seventy between us.

follow_ups.question: How do you organize and track these expenses?
follow_ups.response: I keep a ledger. An actual handwritten ledger, the same kind I've used for fifty years. Every expense goes in there -- the date, what it was, how much. I add it up at the end of the month and compare it to what I budgeted. If we went over somewhere, I figure out where and adjust the next month. Patricia thinks I should use a computer program, but my system works and I'm not changing it now.

transcript.question_text: How much did your household spend in the past month? Is that the usual amount? And if not, how much do you usually spend and on what?
transcript.response: Not counting the property tax, which is a quarterly thing, we probably spent around two thousand dollars last month. That covers groceries, utilities, medications, gas for the car, and incidentals. That's pretty typical for us. In the summer, the heating bill drops but I spend more on garden supplies. Around the holidays, it goes up because of gifts and the bigger family meals. But on average, two thousand is about right. We try hard to keep it consistent, because on a fixed income, surprises are not welcome.

transcript.question_text: Does your household own or rent your home?
transcript.response: We own it. Bought it in 1978, paid off the mortgage in 2003. Best day of my financial life was making that last payment. Now we just have property taxes, insurance, and maintenance. The house needs things from time to time -- a new water heater two years ago, the roof was patched last summer. But at least there's no mortgage hanging over us. Owning this home outright is a big part of how we stay afloat on our income.

follow_ups.question: How do you plan for those bigger home maintenance expenses?
follow_ups.response: I set aside a small amount every month into what I call the house fund. It's not much -- maybe fifty dollars -- but over the course of a year, that's six hundred dollars for repairs. When something bigger comes up, like the roof, I dip into our savings and then rebuild it. Robert helped with the labor on the roof, which saved us quite a bit. I try to anticipate things before they break, which is why I have Harold check things around the house regularly, even if he can't fix them all himself anymore.

transcript.question_text: Bills can fluctuate over the course of a year. Seasons change, there are holidays and special events, school starts and ends, and so on. Tell me all about how your bills have fluctuated over the past year.
transcript.response: The biggest fluctuation is the heating bill. In the dead of winter -- January, February -- our gas bill can be two hundred dollars or more. In the summer it drops down to almost nothing for heat, but then I'm running a fan and the water bill goes up because of the garden. Holidays are another spike. I budget extra for November and December because of Thanksgiving dinner for the family and Christmas gifts. I start setting money aside in September for that. Spring has its own costs -- garden supplies, seeds, potting soil. And then every quarter the property tax hits, which is the big one. I try to smooth it all out by saving a little each month in different envelopes. Harold thinks the envelope system is old-fashioned, but it works.

transcript.question_text: Tell me all about how you coped with any extra expenses in the past months.
transcript.response: The roof repair last summer was the biggest unexpected expense we had recently. That was about eight hundred dollars after Robert did the labor for free. I pulled it from our savings account and then spent the next six months building it back up by cutting our grocery bill where I could -- more soups, more meals from the garden, fewer store-bought extras. I also skipped buying new fabric for a few months, which was hard for me because I love my quilting materials. But priorities are priorities. When an extra expense hits, I tighten everything else until we're back on track.

follow_ups.question: Has there been an unexpected expense that you couldn't handle on your own?
follow_ups.response: Harold's hospital procedure five years ago had some costs we didn't expect. That was when Robert and Patricia stepped in. It was probably a couple of thousand dollars above what the coverage paid. I hated accepting it, but I didn't have a choice. Since then, I've tried to keep our emergency fund a little bigger. I never want to be in that position again if I can help it.

transcript.question_text: Some people have a savings account, some people save in a different way, and some people say they don't save. How about you?
transcript.response: I have a savings account at the local credit union. I've had it for decades. I put money in every month, no matter how small the amount. Even if it's twenty dollars, it goes in. I also keep a small amount of cash in the house for emergencies -- I know they say you shouldn't, but I grew up that way and it makes me feel secure. And then I have my envelope system for specific things: the house fund, the holiday fund, the medical fund. It's all very organized. I can tell you to the penny what's in each envelope and each account. Saving is a habit my parents drilled into me. My father used to say, 'You never know when the rain is coming, so you'd better have something set aside.' I've lived by that my whole life.

follow_ups.question: How has your approach to savings changed now that you're on a fixed income?
follow_ups.response: The amounts are smaller, but the discipline is the same. When Harold was working, we could put away a hundred or more a month. Now it's more like forty or fifty, after everything else is paid. But I never skip a month. Even when things are tight, something goes into savings. It might be ten dollars, but it goes. The total in the account isn't what it used to be -- Harold's procedure and the roof took chunks out of it -- but it's still there, and I sleep better knowing it exists.

transcript.question_text: Some people save for big things, like a home, while others save for a rainy day. How about for you (in the last year)?
transcript.response: At this point in life, I'm saving strictly for rainy days. The big purchases are behind us -- the house is paid for, the car is paid for. Now it's about making sure we have a cushion for medical expenses, home repairs, and the possibility that one of us might need more care someday. That last one is the biggest worry. I've seen what those care facilities cost, and it keeps me up at night. So I save what I can, knowing it probably won't be enough for the worst-case scenario but hoping it covers the bumps along the way.

follow_ups.question: Have you and Harold discussed any specific plans for long-term care?
follow_ups.response: We've talked about it in general terms. We both want to stay in this house as long as possible. Patricia has said she'd move in if it came to that, and I believe her. But I don't want to put that on her. We have a little set aside, and the house itself is an asset. If it really came down to it, we could sell the house and use that money. But I pray it doesn't come to that. This house is our life.

transcript.question_text: Some people have student loans or credit card debt. Others take out loans from family or friends or find other ways of borrowing money. Tell me about all the debts you're paying on right now.
transcript.response: We have no debt. The mortgage is paid off, we don't have credit card debt -- I pay the card in full every month, if we use it at all. No student loans, no car payments. The car is ten years old but it runs fine. I am very proud of the fact that we owe nothing to anyone. My father would turn over in his grave if I carried credit card debt. We live within our means, period. If I can't pay for it outright, I don't buy it.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about any time during the past year that you haven't had enough money to buy something that you needed or pay a bill that was due.
transcript.response: That hasn't happened, knock on wood. I've always managed to cover everything that's due. There have been months where it was tight -- where after all the bills were paid and the groceries were bought, there wasn't much left for anything extra. I delayed buying new fabric for my quilting for a few months because I didn't want to dip into savings for something that's a want, not a need. And there was one month where the electric bill was higher than I expected, and I had to move money from the holiday envelope to cover it. But a bill going unpaid? No. That hasn't happened and I intend to keep it that way.

follow_ups.question: What would you cut first if things ever got that tight?
follow_ups.response: The extras. Fabric, books I could get from the library instead of buying, any meals out -- not that we eat out much, but occasionally Patricia takes us to the diner. Those would go first. Then I'd look at the grocery bill and make more soups, more from-the-garden meals. The last things I'd cut are the medications and the utilities. You can't compromise on health or heat in Michigan.

transcript.question_text: What is or was your occupation? In this occupation, what kind of work do you do and what are the most important activities or duties?
transcript.response: I never had a formal career outside the home. After I got married in 1962, I became a homemaker, and that's what I did for decades. But I want to be clear -- that was a full-time job. I managed the household budget down to the penny, planned every meal, kept the schedules for three children, maintained the house, and did the books for Harold's side carpentry work. I was the treasurer of the women's group at church for over twenty years, and I managed those funds as carefully as if they were my own. When the kids were older, I started volunteering more formally -- the library reading program, the church fellowship coordination, and eventually the senior center finance work. What I do now at the senior center is basically bookkeeping: reconciling donations, tracking expenses, preparing budgets for events, making sure the checking account balances. It's unpaid, but it uses the skills I've honed over a lifetime. If someone had paid me to do what I do at home and as a volunteer, I'd have had a very respectable career in office administration or accounting.

follow_ups.question: Do you think managing a household and doing all that volunteer work gave you skills that are underrecognized?
follow_ups.response: Absolutely. The budgeting, the scheduling, the event planning, the record-keeping -- those are real skills. I've kept ledgers, managed calendars, coordinated dozens of people for events, and never missed a deadline or a payment. In today's world they'd call that project management. Back then they called it being a housewife. I don't say this with bitterness, because I chose that life and I love my family. But I do think women of my generation did a lot of work that was never valued the way it should have been.

follow_ups.question: What about Harold's side carpentry business -- what was your role in that?
follow_ups.response: I kept all the books. Harold would do the work -- building shelves, fixing cabinets, small renovation jobs for neighbors -- and I would track the expenses, send the invoices, follow up on payments. I kept everything in a folder organized by client. It wasn't big money, but it helped supplement his plant income, especially when the kids were young and money was tight. Harold always said he couldn't have done it without me handling the business side.

transcript.question_text: Was your occupation covered by a union or employee association contract?
transcript.response: No, not mine. I was a homemaker and volunteer, so there was no union. Harold's job at the Ford plant was covered by a union, though. He was a member for decades. That union got him good benefits and helped with his retirement. I was grateful for it -- it gave our family stability. But I never had anything like that for myself.

transcript.question_text: In the past year, how many weeks did you work (for a few hours, including paid vacation, paid sick leave, and military service)?
transcript.response: I haven't worked for pay in decades, so zero weeks of paid work. But if you count my volunteer work, I'm at the senior center or the library or doing something for the church almost every week -- probably forty-five weeks out of the year, accounting for holidays and the weeks I was sick. I don't take vacations, really. My 'time off' is just the weeks when something prevents me from going.

transcript.question_text: How many hours each week did you usually work?
transcript.response: For volunteer work, probably eight to ten hours a week between the senior center, the library, and the church coordination. That doesn't count all the hours I spend managing our household, which is a job in itself.

transcript.question_text: In the past week, did you work for pay (even for 1 hour) at a job or business?
transcript.response: No, I didn't.

transcript.question_text: Did you have more than one job or business, including part time, evening, or weekend work?
transcript.response: No. I don't work for pay. My volunteer commitments are at multiple places, but none of them are jobs in the traditional sense.

transcript.question_text: Now, in the past month, how many jobs did you work, including part time, evening, or weekend work?
transcript.response: Zero paid jobs. Just my volunteer work at the senior center on Fridays and the library on Wednesdays.

transcript.question_text: For your job or occupations, how much money did you receive in the past month?
transcript.response: I received no income from work. Our income comes from Harold's retirement pension from Ford, our government retirement benefits, and a small amount from the savings interest. I don't earn wages.

transcript.question_text: How often do you get paid?
transcript.response: The retirement benefits come monthly. Harold's pension check comes monthly too. That's our regular income -- the same amount, same day, every month. I like the predictability of it.

transcript.question_text: In total, how much did your household make in the past month?
transcript.response: Between Harold's pension and both of our government retirement benefits, we bring in about three thousand dollars a month. It's not a lot, but with the mortgage paid off and no debt, it covers our needs. I make it work with careful budgeting. Every dollar has a job.

transcript.question_text: Switching gears a bit, let's talk a little about tax time. Did you file taxes last year (the most recent year)?
transcript.response: Yes, we filed. We always file. I do them myself with the paper forms. I know they have computer programs for it, but I've been filling out the forms for decades and I understand them. Patricia double-checks my math, but I've never made an error that I know of. We usually get a small return, which goes straight into savings.

follow_ups.question: Have you ever considered having someone else do your taxes?
follow_ups.response: People have suggested it, but our taxes aren't complicated -- retirement income, property taxes, a few deductions. It takes me about an afternoon with a calculator and the instruction booklet. I actually enjoy it, if you can believe that. It's numbers, it's organized, it's a puzzle with a right answer. That appeals to me.

transcript.question_text: Tell me more about your workplace. How long have you been at your current job? How would you describe the benefits that come with your job? (This could include things like health benefits, paid time off, vacation, and sick time.)
transcript.response: I'm not employed. My volunteer work has no benefits in the formal sense. No health coverage, no paid time off. The benefits are personal -- staying active, feeling useful, being part of the community.

transcript.question_text: How would you describe your relationships at work? (How is your relationship with your manager or boss? How are your relationships with your coworkers?)
transcript.response: At the senior center, Linda is my main partner and we get along wonderfully -- she appreciates my thoroughness. At the library, the head librarian, Joan, is very kind and organized. We work well together because we both like things done a certain way.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about how predictable your work schedule is. How would you describe your job in terms of flexibility with your hours?
transcript.response: My volunteer schedule is very predictable, because I made it that way. Wednesdays at the library, Fridays at the senior center, church fellowship on the schedule I set myself. It's completely flexible in the sense that I'm a volunteer and could stop at any time, but I treat it like a commitment. If I say I'll be there on Friday at nine, I'm there at eight-fifty. That's how I was raised. The only times I've missed are when I was sick or when the weather was truly dangerous. I don't like canceling. I think reliability is one of the most important qualities a person can have, and at eighty-five, it's one of the things I can still offer.

follow_ups.question: What happens when you do have to cancel?
follow_ups.response: I feel terrible about it. When I was sick last winter and missed three weeks at the library, I called Joan and apologized profusely. She said, 'Deeva, you're a volunteer, not a prisoner,' but it still bothered me. I made up for it by organizing extra supplies when I came back. I always try to make up for lost time, even when nobody expects me to.

transcript.question_text: Some people say it's hard to take or keep a job because of child care. How about for you?
transcript.response: That's not an issue for me now. My children are grown. When they were young, child care was one of the reasons I stayed home rather than getting a paying job. There weren't a lot of affordable options in those days.

transcript.question_text: Do you receive any payments or benefits from SNAP, food stamps, housing voucher payments, supplemental security income, or any other programs like that?
transcript.response: No, we don't receive any of those. We get by on Harold's pension and our government retirement benefits. We've never applied for additional assistance. I know some people need those programs and I'm glad they exist, but we've managed without them. My pride probably wouldn't let me apply even if we qualified, to be honest.

transcript.question_text: Tell me about times over the last year when your income was especially low. And tell me about all the things you did to make ends meet during that time.
transcript.response: Our income doesn't really vary since it's all fixed -- the pension and the retirement benefits come the same amount every month. What varies is the expenses. The months when a big bill hits -- property taxes, a home repair -- those feel like our income is suddenly not enough. When that happens, I cut back on the flexible spending: less at the grocery store, no new fabric or books, simpler meals. I use what's in the pantry and the freezer before I buy more. I also lean on the garden heavily in the summer -- fresh vegetables mean less we need to buy. The tight months usually pass within six to eight weeks, and then we're back to normal.

transcript.question_text: What would it be like for you if you had to spend $400 for an emergency? Would you have the money, and if not, how would you get it?
transcript.response: I could cover it. It would come out of savings and it would hurt, but I could do it without borrowing from anyone. Four hundred dollars is about what I put away in two to three months, so I'd have to rebuild it, but it wouldn't be a crisis. If it were more than that -- say, a couple thousand -- I might need to ask the kids for help, which I would hate to do. But four hundred? Yes, I've got that. That's why I save. That's the whole point of the rainy-day fund.

transcript.question_text: Overall, how do you feel about your financial situation?
transcript.response: I feel cautiously okay. We're not rich by any measure, but we've made smart choices over the decades and we don't owe anyone anything. The house is paid for, the car is paid for, we have some savings. On the other hand, I'm always aware that one big health event could wipe us out. Harold's procedure showed me how fast the bills can pile up, even with coverage. And prices keep going up -- the groceries, the medications, the property taxes -- while our income stays the same. That gap worries me. I try not to dwell on it, but it's always in the back of my mind. I manage it by being disciplined with the budget and not spending on things we don't need. At eighty-five, my biggest financial fear is running out of money before we run out of time. But for now, today, we're alright. And I'm grateful for that.

follow_ups.question: What gives you the most financial peace of mind?
follow_ups.response: Knowing the numbers. I know exactly what comes in and what goes out every month. There are no surprises because I track everything. If I didn't keep the ledger, if I didn't have my envelopes and my system, I'd be a wreck. The control is what keeps me calm. As long as I can see the numbers and they add up, I feel like we're okay. The day I can't manage my own books is the day I'll really start worrying.

follow_ups.question: If you could change one thing about your financial situation, what would it be?
follow_ups.response: I wish the medications were cheaper. That's the one thing that's gone up the most and that we have the least control over. Everything else I can manage -- I can cook cheaper meals, I can delay buying fabric, I can keep the thermostat down. But the pills, you either take them or you don't. And the prices just keep climbing. If someone in charge would do something about that, it would make a real difference for people like Harold and me.

transcript.question_text: Are you now married, widowed, divorced, separated, or have you never been married? If you are not currently married, are you currently living with a romantic partner?
transcript.response: I'm married. Harold and I have been married since 1962 -- sixty-four years this summer. He's the only man I've ever been with, and I can't imagine it any other way. We got married at the church, had a small ceremony with family, and we've been together ever since.

follow_ups.question: What's the secret to sixty-four years of marriage?
follow_ups.response: Routine and respect. We have our roles, we stick to them, and we don't try to change each other. Harold is quiet and steady. I'm organized and a little bossy, if I'm being honest. We fit together like two pieces of a puzzle. We don't agree on everything, but we don't fight about it either. We just get on with it. And we've never gone to bed angry. That's a rule we made early on and we've kept it.

transcript.question_text: For our records, we also need your date of birth. Could you please provide that?
transcript.response: March 14th, 1941. I'll be eighty-six next March, if the good Lord is willing.

transcript.question_text: Were you born in the United States? If you were not born in the U.S., what country were you born, and what year did you first come to the U.S. to live?
transcript.response: Yes, born and raised in the United States. Right here in Michigan. My family has been in Michigan for three generations now.

transcript.question_text: What city and state were you born in?
transcript.response: I was born in Tecumseh, Michigan. Little town about thirty minutes south of where I live now in Saline. Very small, very quiet. Good place to grow up.

transcript.question_text: Are you of Hispanic, Latinx, or Spanish origin?
transcript.response: No, I'm not.

transcript.question_text: What race or races do you identify with?
transcript.response: White. My family is of German descent on both sides.

transcript.question_text: What is the highest degree or grade you've completed?
transcript.response: Some college. I attended community college for about two years studying business administration, but I didn't finish the degree.

transcript.question_text: Are you enrolled in school?
transcript.response: No, not currently. Though I've been thinking about looking into finishing those courses online.

transcript.question_text: Have you been enrolled in school during the past 3 months?
transcript.response: No, I haven't.

transcript.question_text: Have you ever served on active duty in the U.S. Armed Forces, Reserves, or National Guard?
transcript.response: No, I have not.

transcript.question_text: What religion do you identify with, if any?
transcript.response: I've attended the same church here in Saline since 1978. It's where my family goes every Sunday and where I do my volunteer work.

transcript.question_text: Generally speaking, do you usually think of yourself as a Democrat, a Republican, an Independent, or what? And how strongly do you associate with that party?
transcript.response: I'd say I lean toward one side but I don't consider myself a strong partisan. I've split my ticket in the past when I thought the other candidate was better qualified. I pay more attention to the person than the party, especially in local races. I think both sides have gotten too extreme for my taste. I want sensible people who will manage the budget responsibly and look out for folks like Harold and me. I don't go to rallies or put signs in my yard. I just vote my conscience and keep it to myself.

follow_ups.question: Has your party preference shifted at all over the decades?
follow_ups.response: Not dramatically. I've always been practical rather than ideological. I grew up in a household that valued fiscal responsibility and self-reliance, and that still guides my thinking. But I also believe in community programs that help people who need it, like the senior center. So I don't fit perfectly in either box. I vote for the person I think will do the best job, and sometimes that's been one party and sometimes the other.

transcript.question_text: Do you think of yourself as closer to the Democratic Party or to the Republican Party?
transcript.response: I'd say I'm somewhere in the middle but maybe a hair closer to the side that talks about keeping spending in check and letting people run their own lives. But honestly, it depends on the issue. On keeping programs for seniors funded, I side with whoever is protecting those. On government spending overall, I lean toward being careful with taxpayer money. I really don't like being put in a box. I'm just Deeva, and I vote for who I think is the best person for the job.

transcript.question_text: What was the city and state that you lived in when you were 16 years old?
transcript.response: Tecumseh, Michigan. Same town where I was born. I lived there all through school.

transcript.question_text: Did you live with both your own mother and father when you were 16?
transcript.response: Yes, I lived with both my parents. They were together their whole lives. My mother passed in 1995 and my father a few years before that, but when I was sixteen, we were all under one roof.

transcript.question_text: Who else did you live with?
transcript.response: My two older brothers, Walter and Frank. They were a few years older than me. Walter was already thinking about leaving for work by then, but Frank was still at home. It was a full house -- five of us, not counting the dog.

transcript.question_text: What's the highest degree or grade your dad completed?
transcript.response: He finished high school. That was considered a real accomplishment in his family -- his parents hadn't made it past eighth grade.

transcript.question_text: What's the highest degree or grade your mom completed?
transcript.response: She finished high school as well. She was sharp as a tack -- I think she could have done more if the times had been different, but for a woman of that generation, high school was it.

transcript.question_text: Did your mom work for pay for at least a year while you were growing up?
transcript.response: Yes, she did. She worked at the local five-and-dime for a number of years when I was in school.

transcript.question_text: What was her job? What kind of work did she do? What were her most important activities or duties?
transcript.response: She worked the counter at the five-and-dime store in town -- Woolworth's, I believe. She rang up purchases, stocked shelves, kept the display counters tidy. She was good at it because she was organized and friendly with the customers. She knew everyone who came in. It wasn't a glamorous job, but it brought in extra money and she liked being out of the house and around people. She worked there for about eight years, from when I was in elementary school until I started high school.

follow_ups.question: Did your mother's work experience influence your own views on work and managing money?
follow_ups.response: Absolutely. She taught me that every penny counts and that you should always have your own understanding of the family finances. She'd come home from the store and talk about how the owner managed inventory, how much things cost versus how much they sold for. That fascinated me as a girl. I think my love of numbers and budgeting started right there, listening to my mother talk about the store.

transcript.question_text: What kind of place did she work for? What kind of business was it? What did they make or do where she worked?
transcript.response: It was a Woolworth's five-and-dime, a general merchandise store. They sold a little bit of everything -- household goods, candy, school supplies, sewing notions, small hardware items. It was the kind of place where you could get a spool of thread, a coloring book, and a bag of penny candy all in one stop. There was a lunch counter too, though my mother didn't work that part. It was a chain store, but in a small town like Tecumseh, it felt like a local institution. Everyone in town shopped there.

transcript.question_text: Did your dad work for pay for at least a year while you were growing up?
transcript.response: Yes, he worked his whole life. Never missed a day that I can remember.

transcript.question_text: What was his job? What kind of work did he do? What were his most important activities or duties?
transcript.response: My father worked at a grain elevator and feed mill outside of Tecumseh. He ran the equipment that processed and stored grain -- corn, wheat, soybeans. He operated the conveyor systems, checked the moisture levels, managed the storage bins. During harvest season he worked long hours, sometimes twelve-hour days. He was strong, physical work, and it took a toll on his body over the years. But he was proud of it. He'd come home dusty and tired but he never complained. He believed in honest work, and he did it every day for over thirty years.

follow_ups.question: How did your father's work ethic influence you?
follow_ups.response: Enormously. He showed me that you show up, you do the job right, and you don't cut corners. That's exactly how I approach everything, from my quilting to my volunteer work to managing the household budget. When I double-check a column of numbers or go back to fix a stitch that's slightly off, that's my father's voice in my head saying, 'Do it right or don't do it at all.'

transcript.question_text: What kind of place did he work for? What kind of business was it? What did they make or do where he worked?
transcript.response: It was a grain elevator and feed mill -- a local operation that served the farming community in and around Tecumseh. Farmers would bring in their grain after harvest, and the mill would process it, store it, and distribute it. Some of it went to livestock feed, some was shipped out to larger distributors. It was a vital part of the local economy. The owner was a man my father knew from church, and he ran a fair operation. My father started there as a laborer and worked his way up to running the main processing line. It wasn't a big company, but it was steady work, and that's what mattered.

transcript.question_text: We all have hopes about what our future will look like. Imagine yourself a few years from now. Maybe you want your life to be the same in some ways as it is now. Maybe you want it to be different in some ways. What do you hope for?
transcript.response: At eighty-five, I try not to plan too far ahead -- you never know what the Lord has in store. But I hope to keep my independence for as long as possible. I want to stay in this house, keep cooking my own meals, keep going to the senior center and the library and bridge. I want to finish that quilt for the great-grandchild and start the next one. I'd love to finish my college coursework if I can figure out the computer well enough to take the classes. I want Harold and me to have a few more years together, healthy enough to enjoy our routines and our family. I hope to see more of the grandchildren and great-grandchildren, watch them grow up. I'd like to take one more trip to Mackinac Island -- Harold and I used to go there, and it's beautiful in the summer. And I want to visit the National Quilt Museum in Paducah, Kentucky, before I'm too old to travel. That's been on my list for years. Beyond that, I just want peace, stability, and enough good days to outnumber the hard ones.

follow_ups.question: What would a trip to the National Quilt Museum mean to you?
follow_ups.response: It would be a dream come true. I've been quilting for over fifty years, and I've seen pictures of the quilts they have there -- they're masterworks. I want to stand in front of them and study the stitching, the patterns, the colors. Patricia said she'd drive me down there. It would be a road trip, and I'd plan every stop along the way. I'd want to take my time and not rush. At my age, a trip like that is about more than seeing a museum -- it's about proving I can still do something special.

follow_ups.question: If you could pass one thing on to your great-grandchildren, what would it be?
follow_ups.response: The value of order and discipline. I know that sounds boring to a young person, but it's what's carried me through eighty-five years. Keep your finances in order, keep your promises, show up when you said you would, and take care of the people around you. If they learn that, they'll be fine. And I'd want them to know how to make things with their hands -- a quilt, a garden, a good pot pie. There's something about creating with your own two hands that no amount of technology can replace.

transcript.question_text: What do you value the most in your life?
transcript.response: Family, first and always. Harold, my children, my grandchildren, my great-grandchildren -- they're the reason for everything. Second, I value my independence. Being able to manage my own household, my own finances, my own schedule -- that's who I am. The day I can't do that, a big part of me will be gone. Third, I value my community -- the church, the volunteers, the bridge group, the walking club, the people at the library. Those connections keep me going. And I value doing things right. Whether it's a quilt, a budget, a recipe, or a crossword puzzle, I want it done properly. That meticulousness, that attention to detail -- my father gave me that, and it's served me well for eighty-five years. I wouldn't trade it for anything.

follow_ups.question: Is there anything you would do differently if you could go back?
follow_ups.response: I'd finish that degree. That's the one thing I'd change. Everything else -- marrying Harold, having the children, moving to Saline, all of it -- I'd do the same. Well, maybe I'd start quilting earlier. I didn't pick it up until my forties, and I wish I'd had those extra years of practice. But on the big things, the important things? No. I've lived a good life, an honest life, a useful life. I'm grateful for every bit of it.

follow_ups.question: What does a perfect day look like for you at this point in your life?
follow_ups.response: A perfect day is when my knees don't hurt too much, the weather is warm enough to be in the garden, I have something productive to do, and I get to see someone I love. Maybe Patricia stops by, or Dorothy and I take a walk. I cook something that turns out just right. Harold and I sit together in the evening. I work on my quilt for a while. I finish a crossword without looking up any answers. And I go to bed knowing everything is in order for tomorrow. That's a perfect day. Nothing fancy. Just everything in its place.

summaries.demographer: Deeva Cintron is an 85-year-old white woman of German descent, born and raised in rural Michigan (Tecumseh), now living in Saline, Washtenaw County. She has been married to her husband Harold for 64 years and they have three adult children, seven grandchildren, and three great-grandchildren. She completed some college (business administration) but did not finish due to marriage and family responsibilities, a common trajectory for women of her generation. She has been out of the paid workforce since the early 1960s and identifies as a homemaker and volunteer. The household subsists on a fixed income from a Ford union pension and government retirement benefits, totaling approximately $3,000/month. They own their home outright. Her demographic profile is characteristic of the aging Midwestern white working-class: second/third-generation European immigrant heritage, strong church and civic ties, modest education, long-duration stable marriage, and deep rootedness in a small community.
summaries.behavioral_economist: Deeva exhibits strong loss aversion and a preference for predictability in all financial and lifestyle decisions. Her envelope budgeting system, handwritten ledger, and rigid expense-tracking demonstrate an unusually high need for financial control and certainty. She consistently prioritizes needs over wants (delaying fabric purchases, reducing grocery spending) and frames every expenditure in terms of opportunity cost. Her time preferences are heavily present-oriented in terms of daily structure but she maintains a modest savings buffer for future health emergencies, suggesting a dual orientation. She responds to financial uncertainty by tightening discretionary spending rather than seeking additional income. Peer influence on her financial behavior is minimal; she is internally motivated by the discipline instilled by her parents. Her risk tolerance is very low -- she declined a more effective but expensive medication, preferring the predictable cost of the cheaper alternative. Identity and habit are her primary behavioral drivers: she derives self-worth from being 'the numbers person' and would experience significant distress if she lost the ability to manage her own finances.
summaries.political_scientist: Deeva is a highly engaged civic participant who has voted in every election since turning 21, reflecting a deep sense of civic duty transmitted through family socialization. She consumes local news (Saline Post, Ann Arbor News, broadcast evening news) and avoids partisan cable media, positioning her as an information-moderate. She describes herself as issue-oriented rather than party-loyal, with fiscal conservatism (government spending discipline) as her primary political value, tempered by support for community programs benefiting seniors. She has written to elected officials about prescription drug costs and road infrastructure -- bread-and-butter issues affecting her daily life. On social issues like race and policing, she acknowledges her limited perspective as a white woman in a homogeneous small town and defaults to a 'treat everyone with respect' framework rather than structural analysis. Her political participation is high but conventional (voting, letter-writing) with no protest activity. She expresses growing disillusionment with partisan polarization and the tone of public discourse, a sentiment common among older moderate voters.
summaries.psychologist: Deeva presents a behavioral profile marked by high Conscientiousness (meticulous record-keeping, rigid routines, double-checking, punctuality, planning), moderate-to-low Openness (preference for the familiar, traditional patterns, skepticism of unfamiliar approaches), moderate Agreeableness (community-oriented but with a competitive streak in bridge and a need to be right), moderate-to-low Extraversion (prefers small groups, structured socializing, solitary hobbies, uncomfortable with public displays), and moderate Neuroticism (nighttime rumination, worry about health and finances, difficulty accepting vulnerability, but effective coping through activity and routine). Her primary coping mechanism is behavioral activation -- she manages distress by cleaning, organizing, quilting, or engaging in any productive activity. She exhibits strong self-reliance and resistance to dependency, viewing burden on others as a threat to identity. Her relational style is stable and complementary (with Harold) or structured and reciprocal (with bridge friends, volunteers). She shows emerging grief and existential awareness as a last-surviving member of her generation, processed through faith, routine, and quiet endurance rather than verbal expression. Her self-concept is built around competence, reliability, and usefulness -- the loss of any of these capacities represents her deepest psychological vulnerability.
